'''Mark Flanagan''' (born 4 December 1987)<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.weststigers.com.au/?s=article-display&id=23421|title=Wests Tigers|date=18 January 2024 }}</ref><!--also known by the [[nickname]] of '''"Flash"''', --> is an English former professional [[rugby league]] footballer who played as a {{rlp|lf}} or {{rlp|sr}} forward in the [[Super League]] and the [[NRL]], and the [[England Knights]] at international level.

He played for the [[Wigan Warriors]], [[St Helens R.F.C.|St Helens]] and the [[Salford Red Devils]] in the Super League. He won the [[2014 Super League Grand Final]] with [[St Helens R.F.C.|Saints]], and spent time on loan from the club at the [[Rochdale Hornets]] in the [[RFL Championship|Championship]]. He also played for the [[Wests Tigers]] in the [[National Rugby League]].

==Early life==
Mark Flanagan was born on 4 December 1987 in [[Oldham]], [[Greater Manchester]], England. He is the son of former Great Britain international [[Terry Flanagan (rugby league)|Terry Flanagan]].

Flanagan started his rugby league career at the amateur side [[Saddleworth Rangers]] before joining the [[Bradford Bulls]] when he was 16. He was considered one of the most promising young British players and has caps for England Academy including caps against the Australian Schoolboys in 2006.

==Professional playing career==
[[File:Mark Flanagan.JPG|thumb|right|400px|Flanagan warming up for Wigan]]
===Wigan===
On 4 January 2007 it was announced that Flanagan would join [[Wigan Warriors|Wigan]] on a two-year contract with Wigan, paying £10,900 in compensation to Bradford for the loss of the player.<ref>{{cite web|title=Warriors complete Flanagan swoop|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/rugby_league/super_league/wigan/6230783.stm|website=BBC Sport|access-date=12 December 2017|date=4 January 2007}}</ref> He was signed to provide cover and possibly become a replacement to Australian stand-off [[Trent Barrett]]. Flanagan was the third player that [[Brian Noble (rugby league)|Brian Noble]] had brought from his previous club Bradford, the other players being [[Stuart Fielden]] and [[Michael Withers]]. Flanagan was a regular in the Wigan reserves team. He went to [[Halifax R.L.F.C.|Halifax]] on loan in June 2008 and scored two tries in his first game for the club.

After some impressive performances in the reserves Flanagan made his Wigan début from the bench during the second half of a 22–16 defeat by the [[Crusaders Rugby League|Crusaders RL]] at [[Brewery Field]].<ref name="BBCWDebut">{{cite news|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/rugby_league/8091971.stm
|title=Celtic Crusaders 22–16 Wigan|publisher=news.bbc.co.uk ([[BBC Sport]])|access-date=14 June 2009
|date=13 June 2009}}</ref><ref name="IndependantWDebut">{{cite news|url=https://www.independent.co.uk/sport/general/rugby-league/lupton-leads-crusaders-to-historic-victory-1704718.html|title=Lupton leads Crusaders to historic victory|publisher=www.independent.co.uk ([[The Independent]])|accessdate=14 June 2009|last=Short|first=Paul|location=London|date=14 June 2009}}</ref> He made more appearances for [[Wigan Warriors|Wigan]] putting in some very impressive performances, most noticeably for his defensive effort in the derby loss away at St Helens.

===Wests Tigers===
It was announced 23 September 2009 that Flanagan had signed a 1-year contract with the [[Wests Tigers]], with the Tigers having the option for a further season. Tigers' recruitment manager Warren McDonnell described him as, "a raw talent," and, "a tough no-nonsense player with a strong work ethic."<ref>{{cite news|publisher=Daily Telegraph (Australia)|title=Daine Laurie a Panther, Tigers on signing spree|author=Todd Balym|url=http://www.dailytelegraph.com.au/sport/nrl/daine-laurie-a-panther-tigers-on-signing-spree/story-e6frexnr-1225778823667|accessdate=24 September 2009|date=23 September 2009}}</ref> Flanagan played 21 games in his first season with the Tigers, mostly starting on the bench.

Having been left out of the side for most of 2011, Flanagan made his return late in the season and played in both of the Wests Tigers' semis appearances.

===St Helens===
It was confirmed on 19 July 2011 that St Helens had signed Flanagan on a 2-year contract, he made the move before the start of the 2012 season. The move meant that Flanagan was reunited with Head Coach [[Royce Simmons]] who he previously worked with at West Tigers.

St Helens reached the [[2014 Super League Grand Final]], and Flanagan was selected to play at [[Rugby league positions#Halfback|scrum-half]] in their 14–6 victory over the [[Wigan Warriors]] at [[Old Trafford]].<ref>{{cite web|url = https://www.telegraph.co.uk/sport/rugbyleague/11156623/St-Helens-14-Wigan-Warriors-6-Moment-of-madness-from-Wales-international-Ben-Flower-costs-Wigan-dear.html|title= St Helens 14 Wigan Warriors 6: Moment of madness from Wales international Ben Flower costs Wigan dear|date= 11 October 2014 |work=Daily Telegraph|accessdate=15 October 2014}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url = https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2014/oct/11/st-helens-wigan-super-league-grand-final-match-report|title= St Helens win Grand Final after Wigan's Ben Flower is sent off|date= 11 October 2014 |work=Guardian |accessdate=15 October 2014}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url = https://www.bbc.com/sport/0/rugby-league/29559845|title= St Helens 14–6 Wigan Warriors|date= 11 October 2014 |work=BBC Sport|accessdate=15 October 2014}}</ref><ref>{{cite news|last1=Cartwright|first1=Phil|title=St Helens v Wigan as it happened|url=https://www.bbc.com/sport/live/rugby-league/29416637|accessdate=12 October 2014|work=[[BBC Sport]]|date=11 October 2014}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.independent.co.uk/sport/general/rugby-league/super-league-grand-final--st-helens-vs-wigan-mark-flanagan-takes-pivotal-role-against-saints-fiercest-foes-9788542.html|title=Super League Grand Final – St Helens vs Wigan: Mark Flanagan takes pivotal role against Saints' fiercest foes|last1=Hadfield|first1=Dave|date=10 October 2014|work=The Independent|accessdate=13 October 2014}}</ref>

===Salford===
Flanagan left St Helens at the end of the 2015 season to join Salford, where he was reunited with his former Wests Tigers coach [[Tim Sheens]].

He played in the [[2019 Super League Grand Final]] defeat by [[St Helens R.F.C.|St Helens]] at [[Old Trafford]].<ref name="St Helens give Justin Holbrook the perfect send-off with commanding Grand Final victory over Salford">{{cite news|url=https://www.telegraph.co.uk/rugby-league/2019/10/12/st-helens-vs-salford-red-devils-super-league-grand-final-live/|title=St Helens give Justin Holbrook the perfect send-off with commanding Grand Final victory over Salford|newspaper=Telegraph|date=12 October 2019|accessdate=25 October 2019}}</ref><ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/live/2019/oct/12/st-helens-v-salford-red-devils-super-league-grand-final-live/|title=St Helens 23-6 Salford Red Devils: Super League Grand Final – as it happened|website=The Guardian|date=12 October 2019|last1=Unwin|first1=Will}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.skysports.com/rugby-league/news/12196/11777123/st-helens-win-2019-super-league-league-leaders-shield/|title=St Helens win 2019 Super League League Leaders' shield|website=Sky Sports}}</ref>

On 24 September 2020 Flanaghan confirmed his intention to retire at the end of the 2020 season.<ref>{{cite web|website=Sky Sports|date=24 Sep 2020|accessdate=26 Dec 2020|title=Salford's Mark Flanagan confirms retirement at end of the season|url=https://www.skysports.com/rugby-league/news/12207/12080851/salfords-mark-flanagan-confirms-retirement-at-end-of-the-season}}</ref>
On 17 October 2020, he played in the [[2020 Challenge Cup Final]] defeat for Salford against Leeds at [[Wembley Stadium]].<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/54555957|title=Leeds beat Salford 17-16 after Luke Gale lands late winning drop-goal|work=BBC Sport}}</ref>

==Personal life==
In 2015, Flanagan opened an independent coffee shop with former Saints team mate [[Jon Wilkin]] in [[Manchester]] city centre.<ref>{{cite web|last1=O'Leary|first1=Abigail|title=Top rugby league players turn their hands to making artisan coffee in new business quest|url=http://www.manchestereveningnews.co.uk/news/greater-manchester-news/top-rugby-league-players-turn-9060907|website=Manchester Evening News|accessdate=12 December 2017|date=17 April 2015}}</ref> Mark married in [[Barcelona]] at the iconic [[Santa Maria del Mar, Barcelona|Santa Maria Del Mar]] on 2 December 2017.
Mark is the son of former GB international [[Terry Flanagan (rugby league)|Terry Flanaghan]] [[Order of the British Empire|MBE]] .
